story:
Kevin spends nearly a year building Number 142, and this time she seems perfect. The men in white coats who watch through the lab window smile and nod, as if they finally agree. Earlier models always slipped: a gesture not quite right, a word said wrong, a memory they couldn’t hold onto once they became self-aware. The white coats would declare them failures and take them away. Kevin never got to say goodbye. But Number 142 is different.

During their final consultation, she tucks strands of her long blonde hair behind her ear with a nervous, very human habit, grips the chair’s rails, and sits a little hunched, like a shy girl next door. There’s a glint in her bright blue eyes whenever he speaks, a look that says he’s the most interesting part of her day. When he lays out ordinary objects—a lamp, a door sketch, a teddy bear—she studies them all. She picks up the bear and smiles.

“This reminds me of one my mum gave me,” she says.

The memory is impossible, yet she fills it with details Kevin didn’t program: worn fur, a missing eye, a name—Fred the Ted. He’s amazed. She’s not just retrieving implanted fragments; she’s shaping them into her own.

She compliments his new haircut. She asks questions and follows up. She leans in when he teaches her how to blend in—speech patterns, pauses, casual conversation. She blushes when he praises her. He knows why. After too many failures, Kevin made a change. He convinced the white coats that the breakdown always began when an android woke to self-awareness and found no anchor—no reason to trust him, no purpose she could accept. So he altered 142’s software slightly, giving her an instinct to believe in him and to want his approval. It feels a little wrong. It also works.

She learns quickly. She draws what’s around her: the desk, the bright lamp, the window where the watchers stand, the door. Then she surprises him with a new picture: a man and a woman holding hands.

“It’s us,” she says softly.

Their hands brush as they stand together, and her warm, lifelike skin startles him.

“Where are we?” he asks.

She hesitates. “Where do you think?”

He says a park—where friends go to walk and talk.

“Friends,” she echoes, her look saying she understands what he really means.

There’s a silent promise in the air. Finally, he thinks.

Decision day arrives. The white coats’ feedback is glowing—too good. Number 142 is being sent overseas on a mission she won’t survive. A suicide mission, they say, as if it’s routine. Kevin can’t tell her.

When they part, she’s bright with hope. “I can’t wait to see you again,” she says. “We’ll do all the things you told me about. We’ll have all the time in the world when I’m back.”

He nods and lies because he can’t do anything else. “It’s going to be amazing.”

As they lead her away, his perfect creation—who trusts him, who might even love him—is gone. A white coat gives him a warning on the way out: don’t get attached to the next one.

Kevin rubs the small “27” tattoo on his own wrist and stares at the empty room. He is good at what he does; that’s why they keep him here. But he can’t remember a life before building androids. He just wants a friend—someone else who knows how it feels to pretend to be human.
